quiz.title: Unit 7 Quiz 11, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Advanced Applications: Mining, Gases, and Molar Volume
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Ore Processing Calculations, Percent Composition in Minerals, Gas Volume at STP, Molecules to Mass Conversions, Atoms per Molecule Calculations

questions.question:
Copper is often mined from Chalcocite ($Cu_2S$), a dark mineral. Most copper ores are actually very 'lean,' meaning the valuable mineral is only a tiny fraction of the rock.

A low-grade copper ore contains 2.25% Chalcocite ($Cu_2S$) by mass. How many kilograms ($kg$) of this ore must be processed to obtain 500 grams of pure copper metal?

(Atomic masses: $Cu = 63.55$, $S = 32.06$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the mass percentage of copper in $Cu_2S$

Molar mass $Cu_2S = (2 \times 63.55) + 32.06 = 127.10 + 32.06 = 159.16$ g/mol
Fraction Cu = $127.10 / 159.16 = 0.7986$ (79.86%)

Step 2: Calculate mass of $Cu_2S$ needed

Mass $Cu_2S = 500 \text{ g Cu} / 0.7986 = 626.1$ g

Step 3: Calculate total ore mass

$0.0225 \times X = 626.1$ g
$X = 626.1 / 0.0225 = 27,826.7$ g

Step 4: Convert to kilograms

$27,826.7 \text{ g} = 27.83$ kg

questions.question:
Hematite is one of the most important ores of iron. In a blast furnace, the oxygen is removed to leave behind pure liquid iron.

A shipment of iron ore is found to contain 15.5% Hematite ($Fe_2O_3$) by weight. A steel mill needs to produce 2.0 metric tons ($2000\text{ kg}$) of pure iron. How many kilograms of the raw ore must be processed to meet this requirement?

(Atomic masses: $Fe = 55.85$, $O = 16.00$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the mass percentage of iron in $Fe_2O_3$

Molar mass $Fe_2O_3 = (2 \times 55.85) + (3 \times 16.00) = 111.70 + 48.00 = 159.70$ g/mol
Fraction Fe = $111.70 / 159.70 = 0.6994$ (69.94%)

Step 2: Calculate mass of $Fe_2O_3$ needed

Mass $Fe_2O_3 = 2000 \text{ kg Fe} / 0.6994 = 2859.6$ kg

Step 3: Calculate total ore mass

$0.155 \times X = 2859.6$ kg
$X = 2859.6 / 0.155 = 18,449$ kg

questions.question: What is the molarity of a solution containing 4.9 g of H₂SO₄ in 100 cm³ of its solution?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the molar mass of H₂SO₄

H: $2 \times 1.008 = 2.016$ g/mol
S: $32.06$ g/mol
O: $4 \times 16.00 = 64.00$ g/mol
Total = $2.016 + 32.06 + 64.00 = 98.076$ g/mol

Step 2: Calculate the number of moles

Moles = $\frac{4.9 \text{ g}}{98.076 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.05$ mol

Step 3: Convert volume to litres

Volume = $100 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.100 \text{ L}$

Step 4: Calculate molarity

Molarity = $\frac{\text{Moles}}{\text{Volume in L}} = \frac{0.05 \text{ mol}}{0.100 \text{ L}} = 0.50$ M

Therefore, the molarity of the solution is 0.50 M.

questions.question: Calculate the molar concentration of a sodium hydroxide ($NaOH$) solution with a mass-volume percentage of 4% ($w/v$).
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Understand the meaning of 4% (w/v)

A 4% (w/v) solution means 4 grams of solute are dissolved in 100 mL of solution.

Step 2: Find the mass of NaOH in 1 litre (1000 mL)

If 100 mL contains 4 g, then 1000 mL contains:

$4 \text{ g} \times \frac{1000 \text{ mL}}{100 \text{ mL}} = 40 \text{ g}$

Step 3: Calculate the molar mass of NaOH

Na: $22.99$ g/mol
O: $16.00$ g/mol
H: $1.008$ g/mol
Total = $22.99 + 16.00 + 1.008 = 40.00$ g/mol

Step 4: Calculate moles of NaOH in 1 litre

Moles = $\frac{40 \text{ g}}{40.00 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.0$ mol

Step 5: Calculate molarity

Molarity = $\frac{1.0 \text{ mol}}{1.0 \text{ L}} = 1.0$ M

Therefore, the molar concentration of the NaOH solution is 1.0 M.

questions.question:
An ore contains 1.34% of the mineral Argentite ($Ag_2S$) by weight. How many grams of this ore would have to be processed in order to obtain 1 g of pure silver?

(Atomic masses: $Ag = 107.87$, $S = 32.06$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the mass percentage of silver in $Ag_2S$

Molar mass $Ag_2S = (2 \times 107.87) + 32.06 = 215.74 + 32.06 = 247.80$ g/mol
Fraction Ag = $215.74 / 247.80 = 0.8706$ (87.06%)

Step 2: Calculate mass of $Ag_2S$ needed

Mass $Ag_2S = 1 \text{ g Ag} / 0.8706 = 1.1486$ g

Step 3: Calculate total ore mass

$0.0134 \times X = 1.1486$ g
$X = 1.1486 / 0.0134 = 85.72$ g

questions.question: If the atomic weight of nitrogen is expressed as 14 amu, how many atoms would it represent?
questions.explanation: When atomic weight is expressed in atomic mass units (amu), it represents the mass of a single atom. 14 amu is the mass of one nitrogen atom. 14 grams would represent one mole ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms).

questions.question: If the atomic weight of nitrogen is expressed as 14 grams, how many atoms would it represent?
questions.explanation: When atomic weight is expressed in grams, it represents the mass of one mole of atoms. 14 grams of nitrogen contains $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms (Avogadro's number).

questions.question:
What is the mass of $3.01 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of ammonia ($NH_3$)?

(Atomic masses: $N = 14.01$, $H = 1.008$; Avogadro's Number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Convert molecules to moles

Moles = $(3.01 \times 10^{23}) / (6.022 \times 10^{23}) = 0.5$ mol

Step 2: Calculate molar mass of $NH_3$

$NH_3 = 14.01 + (3 \times 1.008) = 14.01 + 3.024 = 17.03$ g/mol

Step 3: Calculate mass

Mass = $0.5 \text{ mol} \times 17.03 \text{ g/mol} = 8.515$ g ≈ $8.52$ g

questions.question:
'Dry ice' is the solid form of carbon dioxide. Imagine you have a small piece of dry ice that contains $1.204 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.

What is the mass of this piece of dry ice in grams?

(Atomic masses: $C = 12.01$, $O = 16.00$; Avogadro's Number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Convert molecules to moles

Moles = $(1.204 \times 10^{24}) / (6.022 \times 10^{23}) = 2.0$ mol

Step 2: Calculate molar mass of $CO_2$

$CO_2 = 12.01 + (2 \times 16.00) = 12.01 + 32.00 = 44.01$ g/mol

Step 3: Calculate mass

Mass = $2.0 \text{ mol} \times 44.01 \text{ g/mol} = 88.02$ g

questions.question:
How many molecules and atoms are present in 14 litres of Chlorine gas ($Cl_2$) at standard temperature and pressure (STP)?

(Molar Volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol; Avogadro's Number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles

Moles = $14 \text{ L} / 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 0.625$ mol

Step 2: Calculate molecules

Molecules = $0.625 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.76 \times 10^{23}$ molecules $Cl_2$

Step 3: Calculate atoms

Each $Cl_2$ molecule contains 2 atoms
Atoms = $3.76 \times 10^{23} \times 2 = 7.52 \times 10^{23}$ atoms

questions.question:
Phosphine ($PH_3$) is a gas used in the semiconductor industry. Suppose a storage cylinder contains 5.6 litres of Phosphine gas at STP.

How many molecules of $PH_3$ are in the cylinder?

(Molar Volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol; Avogadro's Number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles

Moles = $5.6 \text{ L} / 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 0.25$ mol

Step 2: Calculate molecules

Molecules = $0.25 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.505 \times 10^{23}$ molecules $PH_3$

questions.question:
Phosphine ($PH_3$) is a gas used in the semiconductor industry. Suppose a storage cylinder contains 5.6 litres of Phosphine gas at STP.

How many total atoms (Phosphorus + Hydrogen) are present in the cylinder?

(Molar Volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol; Avogadro's Number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$)
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles

Moles = $5.6 \text{ L} / 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 0.25$ mol

Step 2: Calculate molecules

Molecules = $0.25 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.505 \times 10^{23}$ molecules $PH_3$

Step 3: Calculate total atoms

Each $PH_3$ molecule contains 1 P + 3 H = 4 atoms
Total atoms = $1.505 \times 10^{23} \times 4 = 6.02 \times 10^{23}$ atoms

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered advanced applications of the mole concept! 🧪⛏️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review percent composition and multi-step conversions carefully.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on setting up the correct ratios and unit conversions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review mole concept fundamentals, percent calculations, and Avogadro's number applications.
